title: Move.toml pins pas/ptb to branch 'main', not a commit SHA
description: Both PAS-related Git dependencies are pinned to the branch name 'main' rather than a commit SHA. This makes the build non-reproducible: any future commit on upstream MystenLabs/pas@main silently becomes a new dependency input the next time Move.lock is regenerated. The Move.lock currently pins to b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc, but a `sui move build` with `--force` or a lockfile-less environment would silently pull new upstream code. A malicious or accidental upstream change would be shipped without review.
impact: Supply-chain / reproducibility risk. Any fresh environment that regenerates Move.lock imports the latest upstream; semantics of PAS policy / request / templates could change under Hadron without code review.
recommendation: Replace `rev = "main"` with the exact commit SHA already pinned in Move.lock (`b64f0c58c60888de99ad9c5a72e9f0289aa7b6fc`) for both `pas` and `ptb`. Rotate the SHA explicitly when upgrading.
evidence:
pas = { git = "git@github.com:MystenLabs/pas.git", subdir = "packages/pas", rev = "main" }
ptb = { git = "git@github.com:MystenLabs/pas.git", subdir = "packages/ptb", rev = "main" }

title: Move.toml still declares edition "2024.beta" while new module uses "2024"
description: The root package still sets `edition = "2024.beta"` while the newly added `token_template_pas/Move.toml` correctly uses `edition = "2024"`. `2024.beta` is no longer the recommended edition; the Move Book and recent Sui framework code use `2024`. This is not blocking, but the inconsistency between the two in-repo packages is a code-quality signal.
impact: Minor — `2024.beta` is still accepted by the toolchain, but it deviates from Move 2024 standards and may drop behind future compiler releases.
recommendation: Change to `edition = "2024"` to align with `token_template_pas/Move.toml` and Move Book guidance.
evidence: edition = "2024.beta"

title: is_whitelisted / can_send / can_receive abort on non-PAS coins
description: The three public read-only predicates `is_whitelisted<T>`, `can_send<T>`, `can_receive<T>` unconditionally `dynamic_object_field::borrow` the whitelist DOF keyed by `WhitelistKey<T>`. If coin T is not a PAS coin (DOF never created), the borrow aborts with a generic dynamic-object-field error. These are `public`, so external consumers (indexers, scripts, composing contracts) calling them on any non-PAS coin hit an opaque abort instead of receiving `false`.
impact: Abort-on-query rather than a clean `false` return. Breaks composability — any caller that generically queries whitelist state for a coin type without first checking `is_pas_coin` will abort. `assert_can_send`/`assert_can_receive` inherit the same foot-gun.
recommendation: Guard each predicate with an early `if (!is_pas_coin<T>(treasury)) return false;` (or assert `ENotPasCoin`). The choice depends on intent — for read queries returning false is more composable, for asserts hard-fail with `ENotPasCoin` is cleaner than a DOF abort.

title: Whitelist mutators auth-check only is_coin_registered, not is_pas_coin
description: `add_to_whitelist`, `remove_from_whitelist`, `set_whitelist_send`, `set_whitelist_receive` all take `&Auth<WhitelistManagePermission, T>`. `auth::new_auth<P, T>` only asserts `is_coin_registered<T>()` (TreasuryCapKey exists), not `is_pas_coin<T>()` (PolicyCapKey exists). A regulated (non-PAS) coin passes the auth check; the call then reaches `treasury.add_to_whitelist<T>` which `dynamic_object_field::borrow_mut` on a missing WhitelistKey and aborts with a generic DOF error rather than a clean `ENotPasCoin`.
impact: Opaque abort instead of a clean error. Operationally annoying; not a security bypass (the final DOF borrow does abort), but makes debugging harder and signals a missing invariant check.
recommendation: In each whitelist mutator in `pas_admin` (or at top of the matching `treasury.add_to_whitelist<T>` / `remove_from_whitelist<T>` / `set_whitelist_*<T>` functions) add `assert!(treasury.is_pas_coin<T>(), ENotPasCoin);` before mutating the DOF.

title: register_pas_asset lacks early is_pas_coin guard — opaque double-registration abort
description: The function does not call `treasury.is_pas_coin<T>()` at entry. If invoked twice, the first abort comes from upstream `pas::policy::new_for_currency` (`EPolicyAlreadyExists`) or, if T was previously registered as a regulated coin, from Hadron's `ETreasuryCapNotEmpty` / `ECoinAlreadyRegistered` deep in `add_pas_coin_caps`, after the PAS Policy / template command has already been created (and `policy.share()` scheduled). Because the whole tx aborts this is currently safe, but it wastes compute on work that will be rolled back and produces opaque errors.
impact: Operational UX — callers get an error from deep in PAS code instead of a clean Hadron-level abort. Not a security issue because atomic rollback cleans state.
recommendation: Add `assert!(!treasury.is_pas_coin<T>(), EAlreadyRegistered);` (and/or `!treasury.is_coin_registered<T>()`) as the first line after `auth.assert_is_valid_for_treasury`.

title: register_pas_asset does not call version.check_is_valid() directly
description: The function takes `version: &Version` but never calls `version.check_is_valid()` on it. The guarantee comes only from `auth.assert_is_valid_for_treasury` being paired with `auth::new_treasury_auth` which did check version at creation. This depends on the contract that treasury auths are created in the same PTB — which is true today but is an implicit invariant. Downstream modules like `supply.move` follow the same pattern and this is consistent with existing Hadron style, however the function signature explicitly accepts `version` implying it is used, yet it is only passed through to `register_transfer_template` which also does not call `check_is_valid`.
impact: If a future refactor lets a caller re-use an older Auth across a version-mismatch boundary, this function would execute in a blocked version. Low risk today.
recommendation: Add `version.check_is_valid();` as the second statement (after the auth assert) for defense-in-depth, mirroring `pas_transfer::approve_transfer`.

title: pas_supply::mint / burn / seize skip explicit version.check_is_valid()
description: None of the three `pas_supply` entrypoints (`mint`, `burn`, `seize`) takes or checks a `&Version`. They rely on (a) Auth created via `auth::new_auth` having checked version at creation time, and (b) upstream pas policy resolve functions (`clawback_funds::resolve`) asserting via `policy.versioning().assert_is_valid_version()`. Both hold today, but this is a coupled invariant — if `new_auth`'s version check was removed, `mint` would run version-blind. `pas_transfer::approve_transfer` does take and check `&Version` explicitly; the asymmetry is a code smell.
impact: Low — defense-in-depth gap, not an exploit today.
recommendation: Either (a) add `version: &Version` parameter and explicit `version.check_is_valid()` at top of `mint`/`burn`/`seize` for consistency with `pas_transfer`, or (b) document the implicit invariant in the module header.

title: pas_transfer::approve_transfer uses mint/redeem pause flag to gate transfers
description: `approve_transfer` asserts `!treasury.is_mint_redeem_paused<T>()`. This conflates three distinct operations — mint, redeem, transfer — under a single pause flag. The Hadron design document separates application pause (mint/redeem) from protocol pause (`GlobalPausePermission` via DenyCapV2). For PAS coins there is no DenyCapV2 (no regulated compliance), so pausing transfers requires abusing the mint/redeem pause. A treasurer who wants to pause only minting will also block all PAS transfers — which may be unintended.
impact: Operationally surprising — a compliance officer toggling mint pause silently halts secondary market flow. Also the error message `EMintRedeemPaused` confuses users who were attempting a transfer, not a mint.
recommendation: Introduce a dedicated `PasTransferPaused<T>` flag (or a combined `assert_transfer_enabled`). Alternatively, keep the conflation but document it prominently in the module header and use an error code like `ETransfersPaused` distinct from mint/redeem semantics.

title: pas_supply::seize does not require source to be whitelisted — design choice, needs explicit doc
description: `seize` validates `to.owner()` is whitelisted (destination), but does not validate `from` address. This is deliberate per spec (§7.2) so seize can extract funds from a de-whitelisted address. It is not documented in the module or function doc comment; a maintainer could later 'fix' this asymmetry by adding `assert_can_send<T>(from)`, which would break the seize-on-blocked-address flow.
impact: Future maintainer could accidentally add a symmetric whitelist check that would break the compliance clawback path.
recommendation: Add an explicit `/// NOTE: source is intentionally NOT whitelist-gated — seize must work on de-whitelisted addresses.` comment on `seize<T>` (and likewise on `burn<T>`).

title: PAS module approves Request before running assertions
description: `pas_supply::burn` reads `request.data().owner()` and `funds().value()`, then `assert!(amount > 0)`, then `request.approve(ClawbackApproval<T>())`, then `clawback_funds::resolve`. The checks come BEFORE approve. OK here. But `approve_transfer` (pas_transfer.move L48) calls `request.approve(TransferApproval<T>())` AFTER the whitelist / amount asserts — that pattern is correct. In `seize` (L76-93), same order. However the `amount > 0` assert in `burn` protects against burning zero and emitting a spurious `PasBurned` event — but note that `clawback_funds::resolve` itself does not reject zero balances. If `amount == 0`, burn would abort before consuming the request, but since `request` is a hot potato passed by value, the abort correctly rolls back. This is correct; flagging as observation — consider moving the `amount > 0` check as the very first statement before `request.data().owner()` reads, for predictable error order.
impact: No correctness bug; ordering is a minor readability issue.
recommendation: Move `assert!(amount > 0, ...)` up to be consistent with `pas_supply::mint` which asserts amount first.

title: register_transfer_template uses TransferApproval<T>::address_string() for package_id
description: The template's `package_id` is derived from `type_name::with_defining_ids<TransferApproval<T>>().address_string()` — this resolves to the Hadron package address *at registration time*. After a Hadron package upgrade (which produces a new package ID), the registered template continues to reference the OLD package address. All auto-resolved PAS transfers will route to the old version of `hadron::pas_transfer::approve_transfer`, which may have a stale logic or (if the old package is removed / blocked) fail entirely. `update_transfer_template` is provided to remedy this, but its invocation is a manual operational step.
impact: Operational: after package upgrade, transfers silently break or run old logic until an admin calls `update_transfer_template` for every PAS coin. Could be easily missed.
recommendation: (1) Document in `pas_admin`'s module doc header the mandatory post-upgrade runbook: `update_transfer_template<T>` for every registered PAS coin. (2) Consider emitting a `PasTemplateAddressRecorded` event with the captured package address so indexers can detect stale templates. (3) Consider `@hadron`/`current_package()` pattern if available to derive dynamically per-call.

title: Template command argument order mismatches approve_transfer signature
description: The template registers the call as `hadron::pas_transfer::approve_transfer<T>` with arguments `[object_by_id(treasury), ext_input<PAS>("request"), object_by_id(version)]`. But the on-chain Move signature is `approve_transfer<T>(treasury: &Treasury, request: &mut Request<SendFunds<Balance<T>>>, version: &Version)` — so the expected order is `[treasury, request, version]`. The template passes them in that order, which is correct. However, `pas::templates::set_template_command` stores this command keyed by `TransferApproval<T>`, and the PAS autoresolve client uses it to build the PTB. If the Move signature is ever reordered in a future edit (e.g. to insert a parameter), the stored template silently becomes wrong — the BCS-encoded command is opaque on-chain and no compiler check links them.
impact: A future edit that reorders `approve_transfer` parameters (e.g. moving `version` before `request`) compiles cleanly but breaks autoresolve in production. Same risk for renaming the function or module.
recommendation: Add a compile-time-ish guard: e.g. a `#[test_only]` test that round-trips the template through an SDK-style decoder, or a comment above `approve_transfer` that flags it as template-bound and must-not-rename. Prefer using a single module-level constant with the function name and referring to it from both sites.
evidence:
let cmd = ptb::move_call(
        approval_type.address_string().to_string(),
        b"pas_transfer".to_string(),
        b"approve_transfer".to_string(),
        vector[
            ptb::object_by_id(object::id(treasury)),
            ptb::ext_input<PAS>(b"request".to_string()),
            ptb::object_by_id(object::id(version)),
        ],

title: No tests for any new PAS module
description: Neither `pas_admin.move` nor `pas_supply.move` nor `pas_transfer.move` contain any `#[test]` or `#[test_only]` code, and the repo has no `tests/` directory. The PR adds ~300 lines of compliance-critical Move logic — mint/burn/seize/transfer-approve with whitelist gating — with zero unit coverage. Phase-1 modules (e.g. `treasury.move`) only expose `create_for_testing`/`share_for_testing` helpers but none of the new PAS code adds coverage.
impact: No automated guard against regression in the compliance core — e.g. future code that reorders approve/assert calls, or drops a whitelist check, would not be caught.
recommendation: Add unit tests covering at minimum: `mint<T>` happy path + abort cases (paused, non-whitelisted recipient, amount zero, non-PAS coin), `burn<T>` happy + abort (paused, amount zero, version blocked), `seize<T>` happy + abort (source de-whitelisted is OK; destination de-whitelisted aborts), `approve_transfer` happy + abort cases, `register_pas_asset` double-registration.
evidence:
module hadron::pas_admin;

use hadron::auth::{Auth, TreasuryAuth};

title: No tests for pas_supply seize / burn / mint branches
description: Compliance-critical paths in `pas_supply` have no coverage. In particular: the ClawbackApproval witness type is constructed in-module but its `type_name::with_defining_ids` identity must exactly match what `pas_admin::register_pas_asset` registered via `policy.set_required_approval<_, ClawbackApproval<T>>`. A change to the module path or struct layout would silently break `clawback_funds::resolve` at runtime.
impact: Rename/restructure of `ClawbackApproval` would pass Move compilation but fail PAS resolve in production.
recommendation: Add at minimum a round-trip unit test that registers a PAS asset and then burns via `pas_supply::burn`, asserting resolve succeeds. Include a test for `mint<T>` into an unwhitelisted account asserting the expected `ENotWhitelistedReceive` abort.
evidence: public struct ClawbackApproval<phantom T>() has drop;

title: No tests for pas_transfer whitelist / pause / version logic
description: `approve_transfer` implements the permissionless autoresolve entrypoint with whitelist, pause and version checks. There are no tests exercising any branch. Given this is the only code path that actually approves third-party PAS transfers, it is the highest-value testing target in the PR.
impact: Regressions in any branch (pause conflation, whitelist-send vs whitelist-receive mix-up, amount zero, version) would ship unnoticed.
recommendation: Add branch-covering unit tests for `approve_transfer<T>`: (a) both whitelisted — OK; (b) sender not whitelisted — abort `ENotWhitelistedSend`; (c) recipient not whitelisted — abort `ENotWhitelistedReceive`; (d) mint/redeem paused — abort; (e) amount = 0 — abort; (f) version blocked — abort.

title: PAS token template bytecode hardcoded as hex, no regeneration workflow
description: `BYTECODE_HEX` is a long hex blob with no accompanying script or doc for regenerating it from `token_template_pas/sources/token_template.move`. If the upstream `coin_registry` API changes, or if the template source is edited, the bytecode drifts silently from the Move source. There is no CI check that the hex matches a freshly-compiled `token_template.move`.
impact: Operational / build-reproducibility risk. A source edit in `token_template_pas/sources/token_template.move` is not reflected in deployed bytecode. Attacker with repo write could slip inconsistent template bytecode past a source-only review.
recommendation: Either (a) publish bytecode regeneration instructions + a CI check that compiles `token_template_pas` and asserts the resulting bytecode matches this hex, or (b) compile at deploy time via the Move CLI wrapper and drop the hardcoded hex.

title: PAS token template bytecode omits coin_registry (0xc) dependency
description: The bytecode publish lists only `0x1` (std) and `0x2` (sui framework). But `token_template.move` calls `sui::coin_registry::new_currency_with_otw` which returns a `CurrencyInitializer` that must later be finalized via `coin_registry::finalize_registration(&mut CoinRegistry /* 0xc */, ...)`. The publish itself may not need `0xc` (because `new_currency_with_otw` creates the Currency object without touching the registry object in init), but confirm this matches what `sui move build` of `token_template_pas/Move.toml` declares. More importantly, the `deployAndRegisterPasAsset` TS flow does call `coin_registry::finalize_registration` with `0xc` explicitly — so the registry object is needed at runtime but not at publish-time. Verify that the bytecode's `init` does not reach 0xc; if it does, the publish will fail unresolved at runtime.
impact: If 0xc is reached by init, publish aborts with missing dependency. Even if not, the dep list should match the source-level Move.toml for reviewer transparency.
recommendation: Either rebuild bytecode from source and confirm the deps list, or add a comment clarifying why only 0x1/0x2 are needed. For defense-in-depth, include `normalizeSuiObjectId("0xc")` if upstream Sui framework resolves `new_currency_with_otw` against the registry anywhere in its call chain.

title: pkg() returns empty string if deploy-output missing — silent failure
description: `pkg()` returns `CONSTANTS.package.latest || CONSTANTS.package.v1` which defaults to `''` when `deploy-output.json` is absent. No guard warns the operator that the empty string is being passed as a package ID to `@mysten/sui` transaction building. All `pas.ts` entrypoints (`registerPasAsset`, `deployAndRegisterPasAsset`, `addToWhitelist`, etc.) call `pkg()` and pass it into `package: pkg()` arguments. If empty, the SDK will produce an invalid type tag or a confusing RPC error.
impact: Poor operator UX — silent misconfiguration shows up as an opaque SDK error.
recommendation: In `pkg()`, `assert(!!CONSTANTS.package.latest || !!CONSTANTS.package.v1, 'Hadron package not deployed — run `bun run deploy` first')`.

title: mintPasTokens accepts `amount: number` — u64 precision loss > 2^53
description: `mintPasTokens(... , amount: number)` passes a JS `number` into the transaction builder for a u64 amount. For values above 2^53 (9,007,199,254,740,992), precision is lost silently. Institutional token issuance easily exceeds this if decimals are high (e.g. 6 decimals + 10B tokens = 10^19).
impact: Off-by-one or silently-truncated mints at scale.
recommendation: Change `amount: number` → `amount: bigint` and pass `tx.pure.u64(amount)` explicitly (or rely on generated client already doing so). Same fix for any other u64 amount param on the PAS path (confirm generated types from `sui-ts-codegen`).

title: deployAndRegisterPasAsset uses type-substring matching for object-ID extraction
description: Extraction of `treasuryCapObjectId`, `metadataCapObjectId`, `currencyObjectId` uses `t.includes('::coin::TreasuryCap<')` etc. This is fragile: (a) substring match can yield false positives if future Sui framework renames types or adds wrappers, (b) ordering sensitivity — the first matched object wins, even if multiple capabilities are created.
impact: Deploys may mis-extract IDs if framework type names drift; currently low risk because phase-1 regulated flow uses the same pattern.
recommendation: Prefer matching against fully qualified type tags `0x2::coin::TreasuryCap<${coinType}>` once `coinType` is known, or use the object's `objectChanges` structured type.

title: full-setup example uses `{} as any` cast for PASClient suiClient
description: `new PASClient({ suiClient: getClient(NETWORK) as any })` — the `as any` escape hatch hides an SDK-version mismatch between `@mysten/sui@^2.4.0` and whichever Sui client type `@mysten/pas@0.0.3` expects. If the PAS SDK drops `suiClient` and switches to `client`, or the shape of `getClient` changes, this blows up at runtime rather than compile time.
impact: Loses TS type safety at a critical integration boundary.
recommendation: Pin the SDK types properly (upgrade `@mysten/pas` to a version aligned with `@mysten/sui` v2.4.0 types, or drop the `as any` and file an upstream issue). Avoid `any` at the dep boundary.
evidence: const pasClient = new PASClient({ suiClient: getClient(NETWORK) as any });

title: @mysten/pas 0.0.3 — unstable pre-1.0 SDK dependency
description: `@mysten/pas": "^0.0.3"` pins a pre-1.0 SDK with caret semver — any `0.0.x` release will be picked up on `pnpm install`. Combined with the `@mysten/pas` SDK being developmental, automatic patch upgrades can introduce silent API changes.
impact: Build reproducibility risk; surprising breakage from a patch-level upgrade.
recommendation: Pin to an exact version (`"0.0.3"`) and add `@mysten/pas` to a manual upgrade-audit checklist until the SDK hits 1.0.

title: approve_transfer is permissionless and can be spammed to emit PasTransferApproved
description: `approve_transfer` is a public permissionless entrypoint. Anyone can obtain a `Request<SendFunds<Balance<T>>>` by calling `pas::account::send_balance` on an account they own, then call `approve_transfer<T>` repeatedly with zero-address recipients and other permutations. Because `assert_can_send` aborts when sender is not whitelisted, the tx aborts and no event fires — BUT if the attacker is whitelisted (even temporarily), they can splitter-burn gas and emit `PasTransferApproved` events with arbitrary amounts up to their balance. This is standard permissionless-autoresolve behavior and cannot be avoided for the PAS UX.
impact: Event-log spam / indexer noise; no loss of funds.
recommendation: Document in the function doc comment that it is public and permissionless by design, gated only by the whitelist. No code change.

title: Single WhitelistManagePermission covers add + remove + toggle — spec drift
description: The Notion spec (§7.2) calls for `WhitelistAddPermission` and `WhitelistRemovePermission` as separate permissions. The code collapses both (plus set_whitelist_send/receive toggles) into one `WhitelistManagePermission`. This reduces RBAC granularity — an operator role that should only be able to onboard (add) addresses also gains the ability to remove and de-toggle. This may be intentional for simplicity, but it is spec drift.
impact: Coarser RBAC than spec intends. If a regulator or compliance workflow differentiates onboarding vs offboarding responsibilities, this conflation is not possible.
recommendation: Either (a) split into `WhitelistAddPermission`, `WhitelistRemovePermission`, `WhitelistToggleSendPermission`, `WhitelistToggleReceivePermission`, or (b) explicitly document in the PR and spec that the collapse is accepted. Mark the spec as updated.
evidence:
// ============ PAS Whitelist ============

public struct WhitelistManagePermission {}

title: WhitelistEntry is copy+drop+store — accidental value-copy writes
description: `WhitelistEntry { can_send, can_receive }` has `copy, drop, store`. Using `wl[addr].can_send = enabled;` (L345) in `set_whitelist_send` works only because Move 2024 generated index-assign accessors on `Table`. If the Table indexer ever returns by-value (copy) instead of by-mut-ref, the assignment would be a no-op on a discarded copy. With `copy` ability this would compile silently. Currently this is safe because `sui::table` index-assign is mut-ref.
impact: Defense-in-depth — removes the risk that a future refactor accidentally drops the `&mut` borrow semantics.
recommendation: Consider removing `copy` from `WhitelistEntry` (keep `drop, store`). Copy adds no semantic value for a two-bool record stored in a Table.

title: add_to_whitelist defaults both can_send and can_receive to true
description: New whitelist entries default to `{ can_send: true, can_receive: true }`. There is no variant that onboards an address as receive-only (common for newly-created sub-accounts that should accept tokens but not yet send). A compliance officer wanting a receive-only onboarding must add-then-toggle-send-off in two transactions, during which the address is briefly able to send.
impact: Operational window where a newly added address has full send+receive before it is locked down, if the intent was receive-only.
recommendation: Add a variant `add_to_whitelist_with<T>(treasury, addr, can_send, can_receive)` to support atomic onboarding with custom capability bits.

title: set_whitelist_send/receive on un-added address aborts with ENotInWhitelist rather than adding
description: The toggle functions abort if the address is not already in the whitelist. This is arguably correct (prevents accidental onboarding via toggle), but combined with R3-026 it means there is no atomic 'create with specific flags' path. Confirming intended.
impact: Minor ergonomics.
recommendation: No change; document the intended flow (add, then toggle) in the module header.

title: Duplicate EMintAmountZero error constant across supply and pas_supply
description: Both `hadron::supply` and `hadron::pas_supply` define `#[error] const EMintAmountZero: vector<u8>` with identical strings. Clients catching by error code cannot disambiguate which path emitted. Same duplication for `ERedeemAmountZero`/`EBurnAmountZero` (different names but similar semantic).
impact: Minor — debuggability; indexers filtering by module are unaffected.
recommendation: Either unify in a shared `hadron::errors` module, or make PAS-specific names (`EPasMintAmountZero`) to avoid confusion in logs.
evidence:
#[error]
const EMintAmountZero: vector<u8> = b"Mint amount must be greater than zero";

title: PasAssetRegistered event carries no registration context
description: `PasAssetRegistered<T>` is parameterized only by the phantom type — no `treasury_id`, no `policy_id`, no registrant. Indexers cannot tie a PAS registration to the treasury/institution that performed it without correlating with `emit_treasury_created` by timestamp.
impact: Indexer integration friction. For multi-institution deployments this is worse because multiple treasuries can register the same coin type across institutions (subject to pas::policy uniqueness though).
recommendation: Add fields: `treasury_id: ID, policy_id: ID, registrant: address` to `PasAssetRegistered` and pass them at emit site.
evidence: public struct PasAssetRegistered<phantom T> has copy, drop {}

title: WhitelistAdded/Removed/Updated events lack treasury_id
description: `WhitelistAdded<T>`, `WhitelistRemoved<T>`, `WhitelistSendUpdated<T>`, `WhitelistReceiveUpdated<T>` carry only `addr` (and for the updated variants `enabled`). They do not include the `treasury_id`, so indexers tracking compliance across multiple institution treasuries cannot route the event. Role events (L29-39) include `treasury_id` — so there is precedent in the codebase for this shape.
impact: Indexer / compliance-reporting friction.
recommendation: Add `treasury_id: ID` to all PAS whitelist events, following the RoleCreated/RoleAssigned precedent.
